lines.text: Good evening. It is 19:00 Madison time, and if you're hearing this, you made the choice to stay home.
The Purge commenced exactly at the top of the hour. We are live on PRGE, unlicensed and unapologetic, for the next twelve hours.
I'm Quinn Foley. Beside me is Caroline, who's got the weather. Tim's running the board. Holden's got the metrics. We're glad you're here.
Let's start with the baseline: the city is now in legal suspension. What happened outside your door at 18:59 is no longer relevant.
The Capitol's gone dark except for Level-10 personnel. The police have stood down. You are, as they say, on your own.
We'll be here all night. Not to tell you it's going to be fine — that would be a lie I won't tell on this broadcast. But we'll be here.
Caroline Archer, weather. The clock reads 19:00, and I want to set expectations for what you're looking at outside.
Right now: forty-one degrees, mostly clear, southwest wind at eight miles per hour. Visibility is excellent — ten-plus miles.
That changes overnight. We've got a cold front tracking in from the northwest. Should arrive around 01:00 to 01:30, give or take.
When it arrives, temperature drops hard. We're talking low twenties by 04:00. Rain turns to sleet. Visibility craters to maybe two miles.
The practical implication: if you're locked in, you stay locked in. The weather will be working for you until about 05:00.
By sunrise — that's 06:47 AM — we clear out again. Hard frost. The city wakes up to a clean slate, meteorologically speaking.
Holden here. Sports desk's been running pre-Purge analytics all week, so let me give you the historical context for tonight.
Last year at this hour, kill count was sitting at eight. Eight. We were two hours before the first major spike.
East Side neighborhoods accounted for eighteen percent of the night's total activity. That's Atwood, Schenk's Corners — the usual hot zones.
What's interesting is the distribution. Most years, the East Side builds momentum gradually. Last year was different.
Willy Street saw early-night coordination — neighborhood watch got organized by 22:00. That kind of collective response is rare.
Tenney Park was quiet. Consistently quiet. Five kills the entire night. Lowest of any established zone in five years.
The question is whether that pattern repeats or whether we're seeing a shift. We'll know more by midnight, but the baseline is: East Side will be active.
Tim Peplinski, operations. Signal is strong, redundancy is live, and we're broadcasting on our primary frequency.
If you're receiving this, you're on the network. Stay on the network. Don't retransmit — we track that, the feds track that, and both sides get angry.
We'll have field reports coming in as the night develops. Tucker's prepped and moving. You'll hear them live.
Emergency routing updates, evacuation alerts, weather escalations — I'll cut in with those as soon as I have them. No delay.
So here's the thing about hour one. Hour one is always strange because nothing has really happened yet. The night is still potential.
By 21:00, we'll know if tonight is a hot night or a cold night. Right now, we're all holding our breath together.
If you're listening because you're terrified, that's appropriate. If you're listening because you're morbid, that's also appropriate. No judgment here.
We're going to take this an hour at a time. Holden will give you the numbers. Caroline will give you the weather. I'll try to keep us all from losing our minds.
Tim's keeping the signal alive. And Tucker — wherever Tucker is right now — Tucker's going to show us what the actual city looks like when it wakes up.
One more thing on the weather front — if you've got family on the East Side, they're going to see that cold front arrive around the time kill activity usually peaks.
Cold weather suppresses activity. We saw that last year — a measurable dip between 02:00 and 04:00 when the sleet was heaviest.
Not a guarantee. Not a survival tactic. But it's a pattern. And patterns are what we have when we have nothing else.
That ties into the kill-count data perfectly. 2168 showed a thirty-nine percent correlation between sleet duration and reduced East Side activity in hours 02:00 through 04:00.
Theoretically, that's because people are indoors. Practically, we can't prove causation from activity data alone.
But if you're in Atwood tonight and the weather deteriorates, that's actually a sign the night might be getting easier, not harder.
I'm going to level with everyone listening — I've done this seventeen times. Seventeen Purge broadcasts.
Every single year, I start at hour one thinking, 'This is the year we see something different.' This is the year the system breaks or changes or matters.
And every single year, by 04:00 AM, I'm back to pouring whiskey and telling stories about people who don't exist anymore.
But we show up. The listeners show up. We all show up and we sit in this for twelve hours together. That's the broadcast. That's what we do.
Tucker just radioed in from the southwest sector. Preparing for first field deployment. I'll relay once they establish position.
Standard protocol: we get one report per hour minimum, more if the situation escalates. Tucker'll cut audio live — you'll hear it happen.
Before we move into the regular show flow, I want to note something about Willy Street specifically.
The neighborhood watch that formed last year at the Yahara River bridge — that's 2100-block of Williamson — they kept kill count in that perimeter at zero.
Zero. In a twelve-hour Purge night, with organized groups moving through surrounding zones. It's statistically anomalous.
The question is whether they're doing it again tonight. If they are, we might see the same pattern. If they're not, Willy Street reverts to average.
And if they're defending the bridge again, the wind's in their favor this year. Southwest now, but it'll shift northwest when the front moves in.
That means visibility from their position degrades before they have to worry about incoming activity. They get four to five hours of good sightlines.
You're listening to PRGE, Purge Radio, hour one of twelve. The time is 19:00 and the Purge has officially begun.
We're going to be with you all night. Real numbers. Real weather. Real people reporting in as this unfolds.
I've got a drink here, a bucket of them actually, and I promise you the quality of my analysis will not improve as the night goes on.
But honestly? That's the point. We're all exhausted already and it hasn't even started. Let's just survive this together.
I'm setting up the emergency routing map. If things deteriorate in any particular zone, I'll have alternative exit data live within sixty seconds.
Not that you're going anywhere tonight — most of you are locked in solid and that's the right call. But we'll have it if we need it.
Statistically, tonight is just going to be another year. We're not trending toward more or fewer incidents. We're stable at a baseline of approximately 187 kills.
That's not comfort. That's just math. But it's consistent math, and consistency is something.
The weather window I'm watching is hours 02:00 through 05:00. That's when the front occupies Madison proper. After that, clear skies.
If you're a listener in Tenney Park — near the water — that front's going to hit you first. Prepare for wind and visibility loss around 00:45.
We've got commercials coming up in a moment. Actual services for actual Purge-night needs. Listen to them — they're useful.
We'll be right back. Hour one of PRGE, 19:00 Madison. The Purge is happening. You're home. That was the right call.
Quick note before commercial break: East Side neighborhoods specifically have early-night patterns we can track.
If you look at Atwood Avenue, activity typically builds between 20:00 and 21:00. Schenk's Corners follows about thirty minutes later.
By 22:00, both zones are usually in secondary phase. So the next ninety minutes are predictive for the entire East Side night.
That early window is still clear weather, so if anything happens, conditions will be optimal for it. Visibility, temperature — all favorable.
After the front, we get suppression. But the first two hours? That's clean air, clear sightlines, and unfortunately, that's usually when early-night activity happens.
If we get an early spike in the East Side, I'm prepared to reroute emergency comms through the secondary network. Standard protocol.
Listeners won't hear a difference — the broadcast stays live — but we can handle surge traffic if it happens.
I used to live in Atwood. Years ago. Before the scandal, before I became a ghost on the radio.
I lived in a house on North Street. Nothing fancy. Just a place where I could pretend to be normal.
That's not relevant to anything except that it is. It's always relevant. You can't talk about these neighborhoods without remembering the people who wanted to stay in them.
Atwood has seen annual growth in Purge activity for four consecutive years. 2165 through 2168. Consistent five to eight percent YoY increase.
That suggests either increased population density, changing group dynamics, or weapon availability in that zone. Probably combination of all three.
And neighborhoods further north — the Sherman area — have been declining in activity. Lowest counts in a decade last year.
No one's figured out why. It's possible there's a territorial agreement we're not aware of. Or just luck.
You know what I miss most? The ability to just drive through a neighborhood and not think about death statistics.
To see Atwood Avenue and think about the coffee shop on the corner instead of the fire that took three buildings in 2161.
That's the real Purge. Not the night itself. It's what the night does to how you see the city the other 364 days.
We're about four minutes from commercial block. I've got service ads lined up — PurgeShield, MorningCare, the usual rotation.
These aren't just ads. They're resources. Listen to them. They're paying us to stay on the air, and we're using that to keep broadcasting to you.
If I had to predict right now — and I'm making a prediction at 19:00, which is premature and statistically unreliable — I'd say tonight tracks toward average.
Maybe slight uptick in the East Side. Maybe a few more incidents than 2168. But nothing that deviates significantly from the baseline.
Which sounds calm until you remember the baseline is 187 people dead by this time tomorrow.
Weather remains stable for another two hours. If you're going to make any moves — and again, you shouldn't be — now is when you'd do it.
After 21:00, cloud cover increases. After 23:00, sleet. After 01:00, visibility tanks. By then, you want to be where you're staying.
That's hour one. We're one hour into the Purge. Eleven hours remaining.
You're listening to PRGE. We're here. Barricade your doors if you haven't already. Listen to us instead of the panic in your head.
Commercial break. We'll be back with hour two setup.

tickerItems.text: East Side kill count estimated at 0-2 at 19:15. Early-night baseline establishing. Watch for acceleration 20:00–21:00.
Atwood Avenue: residential barricading reported complete. Three blocks south of North Street showing organized watch activity.
Cold front ETA now locked at 01:12–01:28. Wind shift to northwest confirmed by satellite. Visibility drop predictable and measurable.
Willy Street neighborhood watch is mobilizing. Perimeter establishment reported near Yahara River bridge. Last year's pattern may repeat.
PurgeShield: Early-night panel checks for Atwood and Schenk's Corners. We install reinforcement until 20:45. Call now.
Tenney Park residents advised: cold front arrives your zone first around 00:45. Prepare for wind gust and visibility loss.
Text in from 'Sarah, Shorewood': Are you tracking Lake Mendota shoreline incidents? Asking for a friend. We're hearing sounds from the water.
Sherman Avenue North: Maintaining 10-year-low activity pattern. Status: quiet. Reason: classified (by lack of data).
